The standards set by the United States Agricultural Marketing Service (AMS) provide guidelines for the quality and grading of processed fruits and vegetables. These standards are essential for ensuring consistency and quality in the food supply chain, facilitating trade, and protecting consumers.
Key Points from the 1959 Standards
Grading Criteria: The standards include specific criteria for evaluating the quality of processed fruits and vegetables, such as color, texture, flavor, and the absence of defects. Each product category has its own set of standards to ensure uniformity.
Classification: Products are classified into different grades (e.g., U.S. Fancy, U.S. No. 1, U.S. No. 2) based on their quality. Higher grades indicate superior quality, while lower grades may have more defects or lower quality characteristics.
Regulatory Framework: The standards are part of the broader regulatory framework established by the USDA to promote fair trading practices and protect consumers. They are periodically reviewed and updated to reflect changes in industry practices and consumer preferences.
